revision.comment: fixing the bolding
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.comment: mainspace to namespace
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})
{{Ninety-Nine Ōji}}

== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})
{{Ninety-Nine Ōji}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 阪南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Hannan City)|ja|阪南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (阪南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-阪南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] ==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子}}) - '''Jizōdō Ōji''' - '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子}})

The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
