revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)#長岡王子|12=draft}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (和歌山市)#中山王子|12=draft}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)#長岡王子|12=draft}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (和歌山市)#中山王子|12=draft}})

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)#長岡王子|12=draft}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (和歌山市)#中山王子|12=draft}})

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->

==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hannan City|ja|阪南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--阪南市)-->
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Hannan, Osaka|ja|阪南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=draft}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=draft}}. At an intersection near {{ill|Izumi-Tottori Station|ja|和泉鳥取駅|12=draft}}, follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=draft}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=draft}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the {{ill|Hanwa Line|ja|阪和線|12=draft}} crosses the river just north of {{ill|Yamanakadani Station|ja|山中渓駅|12=draft}}.
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}}'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=draft}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=draft}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.text:
本項目では、[[大阪府]][[阪南市]]内に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府阪南市内に推定される王子を扱う。
== 九十九王子 == 阪南市内の九十九王子は2社。
=== 地蔵堂王子 === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|地蔵堂王子跡]] 信達王子から南西に1.5km進んで山中川の流れに行き当たり、[[和泉鳥取駅]]の近くの交差点から、山中川右岸に沿う小道を500mほど行くと、川がくの字に曲がる辺りが崖になっている。この崖は、古くは琵琶ヶ崖と呼ばれ、地蔵堂が崖の南側にあったと言われており（『大阪府史蹟名称天然記念物』<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">長谷川[2007:52]。</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">西[1987: 90]。</ref>）、近くの住宅街の中に標示板が設けられている。
早い時期に退転したものと見られ、山中村地福寺に移され（『和泉名所図会』<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>）、子安地蔵として祀られた<ref name="Nishi_90"/>ものが現存している。
=== 馬目王子 === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|馬目王子跡]] 地蔵堂王子からさらに山中川を上流にさかのぼり、[[山中渓駅]]の北側で[[阪和線]]の線路が山中川を越えた辺りの西側辺りに旧社地がある。ウハ目王子（[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽院]]参詣記・[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月8日条<ref>平凡社[1998: 1510]。</ref>）・宇麻目王子（『[[民経記]]』<ref>平凡社[1998: 1526]。</ref>）などの別称あり。
『[[東鳥取町|東鳥取村]]誌』によれば {{quotation|自然田から山中に通ずる道路側王子原に小祠があった。俗に山中の足神さんとして知られ、いつも大きな草鞋が社前に供えられていた。|『東鳥取村誌』<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}} とある。
[[1908年]]（明治41年）に鳥取神社（阪南市石田）に合祀された後、旧社地は払い下げられ、阪和線の開通時の工事で切り崩されて平地となった。『大阪史蹟名勝天然記念物』によれば、石祠と鳥居が残っていたようだが、石祠のみ、近隣の民家の敷地に移されて祀られているという<ref>長谷川[2007: 54]。</ref>。
== 周辺情報 == 泉南市の長岡王子と地蔵堂王子の間に大阪府下では唯一古道の趣を残している琵琶岸懸がある。 <gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|熊野古道・平安の小径 File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|琵琶岸懸 </gallery>
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社、1998、『大阪府の地名』、平凡社（日本歴史地名大系28） ISBN 458249028X * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1）
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (泉南市)#長岡王子|長岡王子]]） - '''地蔵堂王子''' - '''馬目王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (和歌山市)#中山王子|中山王子]]）
{{九十九王子}} {{デフォルトソート:くしゆうくおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子|はんなんし]] [[Category:大阪府の神社|くしゆうくおうしはんなんし]] [[Category:阪南市の歴史]]

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)#長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (和歌山市)#中山王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}})

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Ninety-Nine Ōji" to "Kujūku Ōji"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Kujūku Ōji? == {{main|Kujūku Ōji}}
The {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Kujūku Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Kujūku Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Kujūku Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Kujūku Ōji? == {{main|Kujūku Ōji}}
The {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Kujūku Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Kujūku Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Kujūku Ōji? == {{main|Kujūku Ōji}}
The {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Kujūku Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Kujūku Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Kujūku Ōji? == {{main|Kujūku Ōji}}
The {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Kujūku Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Kujūku Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Kujūku Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
