revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|qid=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (泉南市)#長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (和歌山市)#中山王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (泉南市)#長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (和歌山市)#中山王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|qq=|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|qq=|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (泉南市)#長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (和歌山市)#中山王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} ''' ({{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (泉南市)#長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}) * '''Jizōdō Ōji''' * '''Manome Ōji''' - ({{ill|Nakayama Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (和歌山市)#中山王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}})

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (阪南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Hannan|WD=Q819670}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370055]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Hannan, Osaka|Hannan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Hannan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Hannan City == There are two Ōji shrines in Hannan City.
=== Jizōdō Ōji === [[File:地蔵堂王子跡.jpg|thumb|Jizōdō Ōji site]] From {{ill|Shindachi Ōji|ja|信達王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, travel 1.5 km southwest until reaching the flow of the {{ill|Yamanaka River|ja|山中川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At an intersection near [[:en:Izumi-Tottori Station|Izumi-Tottori Station]], follow a small road along the right bank of the Yamanaka River for about 500 meters. There, where the river bends sharply, the area becomes a cliff. This cliff was historically called Biwa-ga-Kake, and it is said that a Jizōdō (Jizō Hall) once stood on the southern side of the cliff (as noted in *Osaka Prefecture Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53">Hasegawa [2007:52]</ref><ref name="Nishi_90">Nishi [1987: 90]</ref>). A marker has been set up in a nearby residential area.
It is believed that this shrine was abandoned at an early date. The deity was later relocated to {{ill|Jifuku-ji Temple|ja|地福寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jifuku-ji Temple|1=Jifuku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16281009}} in Yamanaka Village (*Izumi Meisho Zue*<ref name="Hasegawa_52-53"/>), where it is still worshipped today as {{ill|Koyasu Jizō|ja|子安地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koyasu Jizō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="Nishi_90"/>
=== Manome Ōji === [[File:馬目王子跡.jpg|thumb|Manome Ōji site]] Continuing upstream along the Yamanaka River from Jizōdō Ōji, the old shrine site is located near the point where the [[:en:Hanwa Line|Hanwa Line]] crosses the river just north of [[:en:Yamanakadani Station|Yamanakadani Station]].
This shrine is also known by alternative names such as "Uha-me Ōji" (recorded in Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]]'s pilgrimage account in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), October 8 entry<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1510]</ref>) and "Uma-me Ōji" (recorded in *Minkeiki*<ref>Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>).
According to the *Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*: {{quotation|A small shrine once stood beside the road connecting Shizuden to Yamanaka. It was commonly known as the "Ashigami-san of Yamanaka" (Foot Deity), and large straw sandals were always offered in front of the shrine.|*Higashi-Tottori Village Gazetteer*<ref name="Nishi_90"/>}}
In 1908 (Meiji 41), it was merged into {{ill|Tottori Shrine|ja|鳥取神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tottori Shrine|1=Tottori Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11674833}} in Hannan City, Ishida. The original shrine site was later sold, and when the Hanwa Line was constructed, the land was excavated and leveled. According to *Osaka Historical Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments*, a stone shrine and torii once remained at the site, but now only the stone shrine has been relocated and enshrined within the grounds of a nearby private residence.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 54]</ref>.
== Surrounding Area == Between {{ill|Nagaoka Ōji|ja|長岡王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagaoka Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Sennan City and Jizōdō Ōji in Hannan City lies the Biwa-gake Cliffs, the only area in Osaka Prefecture that still retains the atmosphere of the ancient Kumano pilgrimage road.
<gallery perrow="2" widths="175px" heights="175px"> File:熊野古道紀伊路.jpg|Kumano Kodo - Heian Path File:琵琶岸懸 01.jpg|Biwa-gake Cliffs </gallery>
